From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Governing body for professional rugby league football in England

Not to be confused with [Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union").

"RFL" redirects here. For other uses, see [RFL (disambiguation)](/wiki/RFL_(disambiguation) "RFL (disambiguation)").

The **Rugby Football League** (**RFL**) is the governing body for [rugby league in England](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_England "Rugby league in England").[[3]](#cite_note-4) Founded in 1895 as the **Northern Rugby Football Union** following 22 clubs resigning from the [Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union"), it changed its name in 1922 to the Rugby Football League.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Based at [Sportcity](/wiki/Sportcity "Sportcity") in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), it is responsible for organising professional competitions and, in association with the [British Amateur Rugby League Association](/wiki/British_Amateur_Rugby_League_Association "British Amateur Rugby League Association") (BARLA), the community game. It also provides [match officials](/wiki/RFL_match_officials "RFL match officials") for every level as well as administering the [England national team](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team")

The Rugby Football League has been a member of the [International Rugby League](/wiki/International_Rugby_League "International Rugby League") (IRL) since 1948 and [European Rugby League](/wiki/European_Rugby_League "European Rugby League") (ERL) since its foundation in 2003.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Celebrating_the_birth_of_Rugby_League.jpg/250px-Celebrating_the_birth_of_Rugby_League.jpg)](/wiki/File:Celebrating_the_birth_of_Rugby_League.jpg)

Plaque celebrating the birth of Rugby League

See also: [History of rugby league](/wiki/History_of_rugby_league "History of rugby league")

On Tuesday 27 August 1895, as a result of an emergency meeting in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), prominent Lancashire rugby clubs Broughton Rangers, Leigh, Oldham, Rochdale Hornets, St Helens, Tyldesley, Warrington, Widnes and Wigan declared that they would support their Yorkshire colleagues in their proposal to form a Northern Union.

Two days later, on Thursday 29 August 1895, representatives of 21 clubs met in the [George Hotel](/wiki/George_Hotel,_Huddersfield "George Hotel, Huddersfield"), Huddersfield to form the "Northern Rugby Football Union" (usually termed Northern Union or NU). Twenty clubs agreed to resign from the [Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union"), but Dewsbury felt unable to comply with the decision. The [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire") club, Stockport, had telegraphed the meeting requesting admission to the new organisation and was duly accepted with a second Cheshire club, Runcorn, admitted at the next meeting.

The 22 clubs were:

1. [Batley](/wiki/Batley_Bulldogs "Batley Bulldogs")
2. [Bradford FC](/wiki/Bradford_(Park_Avenue) "Bradford (Park Avenue)") (switched to association football in 1907 and became [Bradford Park Avenue](/wiki/Bradford_(Park_Avenue) "Bradford (Park Avenue)"))[[5]](#cite_note-6)
3. [Brighouse Rangers](/wiki/Brighouse_Rangers "Brighouse Rangers") (Folded in 1906, now survives as an amateur club)
4. [Broughton Rangers](/wiki/Broughton_Rangers "Broughton Rangers") (Folded in 1955)
5. [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_Panthers "Halifax Panthers")
6. [Huddersfield](/wiki/Huddersfield_Giants "Huddersfield Giants")
7. [Hull FC](/wiki/Hull_FC "Hull FC")
8. [Hunslet](/wiki/Hunslet_F.C._(1883) "Hunslet F.C. (1883)") (Folded in 1973 but were resurrected as a [Phoenix club](/wiki/Hunslet_RLFC "Hunslet RLFC"))
9. [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds_Rhinos "Leeds Rhinos")
10. [Leigh](/wiki/Leigh_Leopards "Leigh Leopards")
11. [Liversedge](/wiki/Liversedge_RFC "Liversedge RFC") (Folded in 1902, survive as an amateur club)
12. [Manningham](/wiki/Manningham_FC "Manningham FC") (Switched to association football in 1903 and became [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City "Bradford City"))
13. [Oldham](/wiki/Oldham_RLFC "Oldham RLFC")
14. [Rochdale Hornets](/wiki/Rochdale_Hornets "Rochdale Hornets")
15. [Runcorn](/wiki/Runcorn_RFC "Runcorn RFC") (Folded in 1918)
16. [St Helens](/wiki/St_Helens_RFC "St Helens RFC")
17. [Stockport](/wiki/Stockport_RFC "Stockport RFC") (Folded in 1902)
18. [Tyldesley](/wiki/Tyldesley_FC "Tyldesley FC") (Folded in 1902)
19. [Wakefield Trinity](/wiki/Wakefield_Trinity "Wakefield Trinity")
20. [Warrington](/wiki/Warrington_Wolves "Warrington Wolves")
21. [Widnes](/wiki/Widnes_Vikings "Widnes Vikings")
22. [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors")

### Subsequent events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Subsequent events")]

**Source:**[[6]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Challenge_cup_1897.jpg/250px-Challenge_cup_1897.jpg)](/wiki/File:Challenge_cup_1897.jpg)

The [first Challenge Cup Final](/wiki/1896%E2%80%9397_Northern_Rugby_Football_Union_season#Final "1896–97 Northern Rugby Football Union season") in 1897 was won by [Batley](/wiki/Batley_Bulldogs "Batley Bulldogs") against [St Helens](/wiki/St_Helens_R.F.C. "St Helens R.F.C.")

Upon its founding, the Northern Union created in [league competition](/wiki/Rugby_Football_League_Championship_First_Division "Rugby Football League Championship First Division") with the first season being the [1895–96 season](/wiki/1895%E2%80%9396_Northern_Rugby_Football_Union_season "1895–96 Northern Rugby Football Union season"). The [Challenge Cup](/wiki/Challenge_Cup "Challenge Cup") came two years later in [1896–97](/wiki/1896%E2%80%9397_Northern_Rugby_Football_Union_season "1896–97 Northern Rugby Football Union season").

1904 saw the creation of the [England national rugby league team](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team") who played the [Other Nationalities rugby league team](/wiki/Other_Nationalities_rugby_league_team "Other Nationalities rugby league team") in rugby league's first international matches.

In 1906, the Northern Union reduced the sport from 15-a-side to 13-a-side.

In 1907, the Northern Union's brand of rugby was introduced to New Zealand and saw the first ever [rugby league tours of New Zealand to Great Britain](/wiki/1907%E2%80%9308_New_Zealand_rugby_tour_of_Australia_and_Great_Britain "1907–08 New Zealand rugby tour of Australia and Great Britain"). For this tour the [Wales national rugby league team](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team") was formed with the match between Wales and New Zealand being the first recognised international test match by the [International Rugby League](/wiki/International_Rugby_League "International Rugby League"). On this tour New Zealand played England, and the newly formed [Great Britain national rugby league team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") combining the best players of England and Wales. The Northern Union would go on to favour the use of a Great Britain side over separate England and Wales sides. A year later, the sport was introduced to Australia and saw a [similar tour](/wiki/1908%E2%80%9309_Kangaroo_tour_of_Great_Britain "1908–09 Kangaroo tour of Great Britain") carried out the Australians.

In 1922, Northern Rugby Union became known as "Rugby League".

In 1929, the Challenge Cup final moved to [the national stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)").

In 1933, an [Ashes](/wiki/The_Ashes_(rugby_league) "The Ashes (rugby league)") match was held in France. The following year, with the help of the RFL, the [French Rugby League Federation](/wiki/French_Rugby_League_Federation "French Rugby League Federation") was formed and rugby league reached its fourth country.

In 1954, the first [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup") was held in France and was won by Great Britain.

In 1971, the RFL introduced the 6 tackle rule.

The [British Amateur Rugby League Association](/wiki/British_Amateur_Rugby_League_Association "British Amateur Rugby League Association") (BARLA) was created in 1973 in Huddersfield by a group of enthusiasts concerned about the dramatic disappearance of many amateur leagues and clubs. Fewer than 150 amateur teams remained with a mere 30 youth rugby league teams. The 'breakaway' from the RFL was acrimonious and was strongly contested, with a vote 29–1 against recognising BARLA. Thanks to Tom Mitchell, this changed to a unanimous vote of approval for BARLA within 12 months.

In 1976, the RFL created the [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge"), seeing the British champions play the champions of Australia, the only other major rugby league.

1991 saw the introduced of a formalised youth leagues.

[Maurice Lindsay](/wiki/Maurice_Lindsay_(Rugby_League) "Maurice Lindsay (Rugby League)") became the Chief Executive of the RFL in 1992, proposing the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"), which replaced [Championship](/wiki/Rugby_Football_League_Championship "Rugby Football League Championship") as the sport's premier league competition from 1996 onwards. Lindsay returned to Wigan in 1999 for his second stint at the club after [Sir Rodney Walker](/wiki/Rodney_Walker_(rugby_league) "Rodney Walker (rugby league)"), then chairman of the RFL, sacked him from the RFL after a campaign to unseat him failed.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

In 1995, the RFL appointed its first Development Officer for Ireland with the aim of assisting [Rugby League Ireland](/wiki/Rugby_League_Ireland "Rugby League Ireland") in the sports development across the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea").[[8]](#cite_note-History-9) 1995 also saw the Great Britain side reserved to tours with England and Wales competing separately in the [World Cup](/wiki/1995_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1995 Rugby League World Cup").

1996 saw the switch to summer rugby and the creation of the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"). The competition aimed to be a European competition outside of the [British rugby league system](/wiki/British_rugby_league_system "British rugby league system"), however this never materialised. Despite this, [Paris Saint-Germain F.C.](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C.") formed a [rugby league team](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_Rugby_League "Paris Saint-Germain Rugby League") who competed in the first two seasons, marking the first foreign team to complete in the British game.

The RFL accumulated losses of £1.9 million at the end of 2001, shortly before a major restructuring of the governing body and the appointment of [Richard Lewis](/wiki/Richard_Lewis_(tennis_and_rugby_league) "Richard Lewis (tennis and rugby league)") as executive chairman in May 2002.[[9]](#cite_note-10) Within a year of joining the RFL, he oversaw reunification with BARLA after nearly 30 years of division.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Lewis left in 2012 to become Chief Executive of the [All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club](/wiki/All_England_Lawn_Tennis_and_Croquet_Club "All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club").[[11]](#cite_note-web2012-12) The RFL net value has been positive every year since 2004, being £1.7 million in 2011.[[12]](#cite_note-RFL2011-13)

In 2002, [London Skolars](/wiki/London_Skolars "London Skolars") became the first amateur club to join the professional system, being the first club to do so in 80 years, and marked the start of a period where a number of clubs moved up, each with various levels of success and failure.

In 2006, [Catalans Dragons](/wiki/Catalans_Dragons "Catalans Dragons") joined the British game, marking the second French team to do so. Unlike PSG, Catalans would go on to be a success in the British game.

In 2011 a major change to the game was agreed, changing from a winter to a summer game, starting in 2012 with a playing season from March to November, aligning with the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"), which has played this way since 1996. The regional leagues may include winter competitions in addition.[[12]](#cite_note-RFL2011-13)

In 2012, the Rugby Football League were awarded the [Stonewall Sport Award](/wiki/Stonewall_Awards "Stonewall Awards") in recognition of their work in embracing inclusivity and tackling homophobia.[[13]](#cite_note-14) They also became the first UK sporting organisation to make the top 100 employers in the Stonewall Index that measures attitudes towards lesbian, gay and bisexual staff.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

[Prince Harry, Duke of Sussex](/wiki/Prince_Harry,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Harry, Duke of Sussex") served as patron until February 2021. He was replaced by his sister in law [The Princess of Wales](/wiki/Catherine,_Princess_of_Wales "Catherine, Princess of Wales").[[15]](#cite_note-BBC-final-16)

In March 2025, chairman Simon Johnson underwent a high profile sudden departure following a motion passed at a council meeting by [Leigh Leopards](/wiki/Leigh_Leopards "Leigh Leopards") and [Batley Bulldogs](/wiki/Batley_Bulldogs "Batley Bulldogs").[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) This coincidence with a number of other executives' resignations aimed growing unrest from clubs regarding the governing body's decision making.[[19]](#cite_note-20) [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") describe the RFL as going "from once crisis to another", with this event marking three leadership changes in eight years. The newspaper stated the lack of effective leadership had led to an increase in club power and threatened any potential investments from the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League"), in addition to risking the reversal of the good work that had been on on-field in recent years.[[20]](#cite_note-21) In this time of crisis, multiple whistleblowers made complaints about historical sexism and misogyny within the organisation.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

In August 2025, [Sport England](/wiki/Sport_England "Sport England") required the RFL to come up with a "Governance Action Plan" after concerns relating to compliance with England's codes of sports governance.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)

In an October 2025 meeting between the RFL and the [Australian Rugby League Commission](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_Commission "Australian Rugby League Commission"), ARLC chief [Peter V'landys](/wiki/Peter_V%27landys "Peter V'landys") described the RFLs financial viability as a "train crash". Stating they needed to drastically find ways to increase revenue to support the "strong product" they govern.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

## Responsibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Responsibility")]

The Rugby Football League began in 1895 as a rival governing body for rugby in England (rugby league and rugby union had not yet formally diverged at this time) and operated in [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") only. By 1907, rugby league had begun in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") with Welsh clubs being incorporated into the Northern English league and saw a separate short lived [Wales only league](/wiki/Welsh_League "Welsh League") being run by the RFL. 1909 saw the sport's introduced in Scotland, but did not see the same response as Wales. Despite this, the RFL established itself as the governing body for [rugby league in Great Britain](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_the_British_Isles "Rugby league in the British Isles").

This was the norm until 1994 and the founding of [Scotland Rugby League](/wiki/Scotland_Rugby_League "Scotland Rugby League") and [Wales Rugby League](/wiki/Wales_Rugby_League "Wales Rugby League") a year later. These bodies became the official governing body for Scotland and Wales leaving the RFL as the official governing body for [rugby league in England](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_England "Rugby league in England") only. Despite this, Scotland Rugby League is partnered with the RFL in attempt to aid the sport's development in Scotland,[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[b]](#cite_note-27) and Wales Rugby League shares governance with the RFL over the domestic competition but has full control of the national team.[[2]](#cite_note-wrl.wales-2) The RFL never attempted to govern [rugby league in Ireland](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_Ireland "Rugby league in Ireland") but does assist the sport's development with [Rugby League Ireland](/wiki/Rugby_League_Ireland "Rugby League Ireland") who were formed in 1988.[[8]](#cite_note-History-9)

Internationally, the RFL established the [England national rugby league team](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team") in 1904, and [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team") and [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") in 1907. English, Welsh, Scottish, and Irish players were selected for Great Britain. National teams of Scotland and Ireland were never established under the RFL. The Wales national team has been run by Wales Rugby League since 1995.

## National teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: National teams")]

### Men's national team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Men's national team")]

Main article: [England national rugby league team](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team")

The England national rugby league team represent England in international [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football tournaments. The team has been largely absent from major tournaments from most of history, having been represented instead by the [Great Britain team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team"), who also represented [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_league_team "Scotland national rugby league team"), and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_league_team "Ireland national rugby league team"). The team (along with Wales, Scotland, and Ireland) replaced Great Britain at the [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup") in [1995](/wiki/1995_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1995 Rugby League World Cup") and in all other tours and test matches following the [2007 All Golds tour](/wiki/2007_All_Golds_tour "2007 All Golds tour").[[26]](#cite_note-28)

The team was formed in 1904 when they played against the [Other Nationalities rugby league team](/wiki/Other_Nationalities_rugby_league_team "Other Nationalities rugby league team"), at the time a mixture of Welsh and Scottish players, in [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan "Wigan").[[27]](#cite_note-29) The team contested the [1975 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/1975_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1975 Rugby League World Cup") as a one off due to Wales wanting to showcase their unusually high level of talent at the time (no Scottish or Irish players made the original Great Britain squad), England finish runners-up in that tournament. Since their regular appearances in the World Cup, they have finished as runners-up in a further two occasions, in [1995](/wiki/1995_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1995 Rugby League World Cup") (as hosts) and [2017](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2017 Rugby League World Cup").

England's only major tournament for most of history prior to 1995, England's only tournament was the [Rugby League European Championship](/wiki/Rugby_League_European_Championship "Rugby League European Championship"), in which they developed rivalries with [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team") and [France](/wiki/France_national_rugby_league_team "France national rugby league team"). Since then, England has inherited Great Britain's rivalries, primarily with [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") (to whom they now contest [The Ashes](/wiki/Men%27s_rugby_league_Ashes "Men's rugby league Ashes") instead of Great Britain) as well [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team").

Traditionally a predominantly white kit is worn including white shorts and socks. However the shirt usually features some form of red, like red stripes, crosses or chevrons. These colours are similar to other English sporting teams and are the colours used on the national flag. In 2008 a new kit was introduced featuring a red cross on the front and red strips down the sides of the shirt, shorts and socks were white too with red strips.[[28]](#cite_note-30) In 2008, the Rugby Football League chose to abandon the traditional English lion on the badge in favour of a much simpler shield and [Saint George's Cross](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Cross "Saint George's Cross") design,[[29]](#cite_note-31) nevertheless the team will still be known as "The Lions".

As of September 2024 the team is ranked third in the world, behind [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team").[[30]](#cite_note-32) [Shaun Wane](/wiki/Shaun_Wane "Shaun Wane") is head coach and [George Williams](/wiki/George_Williams_(rugby_league,_born_1994) "George Williams (rugby league, born 1994)") is the current captain.

### Great Britain national team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Great Britain national team")]

Main article: [Great Britain national rugby league team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team")

The Great Britain national rugby league team represents Great Britain in [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football. Administered by the Rugby Football League (RFL), the team is nicknamed the "Great Britain Lions", or simply "The Lions".

For most of the 20th century the Great Britain team was assembled to go on tours overseas, and to play against foreign touring teams, as well as competing in [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup") tournaments. They were one of the strongest teams in rugby league, though usually playing second fiddle to [Australia](/wiki/Australian_Kangaroos "Australian Kangaroos"). They won the [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup") on three occasions: [1954](/wiki/1954_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1954 Rugby League World Cup"), [1960](/wiki/1960_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1960 Rugby League World Cup") and [1972](/wiki/1972_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1972 Rugby League World Cup").

Since [1995](/wiki/1995_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1995 Rugby League World Cup") the RFL have sent the [home nations](/wiki/Home_nations "Home nations") as separate teams for World Cup purposes. Great Britain continued to compete as a test playing nation both home and away. They competed against Australia for [the Ashes](/wiki/The_Ashes_(rugby_league) "The Ashes (rugby league)"), and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team") for the [Baskerville Shield](/wiki/Baskerville_Shield "Baskerville Shield"), as well the [Tri-Nations](/wiki/Rugby_League_Tri-Nations "Rugby League Tri-Nations") series with both Australia and New Zealand. Great Britain also played in series and tours against other nations such as [France](/wiki/France_national_rugby_league_team "France national rugby league team"), [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea_national_rugby_league_team "Papua New Guinea national rugby league team") and [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji_national_rugby_league_team "Fiji national rugby league team").

In 2006, the RFL announced that after the [2007 All Golds Tour](/wiki/2007_All_Golds_Tour "2007 All Golds Tour")[[31]](#cite_note-RLSL-33) the Great Britain team would no longer compete on a regular basis, and that players would be able to represent [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_league_team "Scotland national rugby league team") at Test level.

It was planned that the Great Britain team will come together in future only for occasional tours, similar to the [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions") in [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"). However since then, only one of these tours has occurred, the [2019 Great Britain Lions tour](/wiki/2019_Great_Britain_Lions_tour "2019 Great Britain Lions tour"), which saw the England squad bolstered by a single Scotsman compete.

### England Community Lions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: England Community Lions")]

The England Community Lions are amateur England sides in both men and women's rugby league, with open-age, masters, PDRL, LDRL, and various age category youth teams.

The women's open age category selects player from the [RFL Women's Championship](/wiki/RFL_Women%27s_Championship "RFL Women's Championship") and below, with no player being allowed for than one appearance for the [national team](/wiki/England_women%27s_national_rugby_league_team "England women's national rugby league team").[[32]](#cite_note-34)

The men's open age category selects players from tier 4 of the [British rugby league system](/wiki/British_rugby_league_system "British rugby league system") and below.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

### Young People's Advisory Panel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Young People's Advisory Panel")]

The RFL launched the Young People's Advisory Panel in 2010, a group consisting of young people aged 16–25 from across England. The national panel meet at least three times a year at the RFL's headquarters to discuss and debate the following:

* Changes in the structure of youth rugby;
* Communications between young rugby league enthusiasts and the RFL;
* RFL policies which impact on young people.

Two nominated members will also sit on the youth & junior forum, a key device used to advance youth rugby league.[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37)

## Logo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Logo")]

The first logo used by the RFL was an oval shape, representing the ball with XIII and 13 over it and *The Rugby Football League* around it. The logo was also seen on the sleeve of teams shirts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d7/Rugby_Football_League_2005_logo.svg/250px-Rugby_Football_League_2005_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rugby_Football_League_2005_logo.svg)

RFL logo used until 2017 rebrand

In the late 1990s the logo was changed to a more simplistic design to the old one. It had a rugby ball shape with three small lines, representing 13 players, and two long lines, representing goalposts, arranged on a rugby ball so as to suggest a hand carrying or passing it. The *Rugby Football League* was abbreviated to *RFL*.

In 2017 the RFL had its most radical rebrand since the formation of [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"). The new logo was a rectangular background meant to represent The George Hotel in Huddersfield, where rugby league was founded and 1895 the year it was founded. Thirteen stripes inside it represent thirteen players. The oval on top represents the ball and the appreciation RFL has been replaced with *Rugby Football League*.

## Headquarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_Football_League&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Headquarters")]

The RFLs current headquarters are at [Sportcity](/wiki/Sportcity "Sportcity") Eithad Campus in East Manchester. The RFL first moved into permanent headquarters in 1922 at 180 Chapeltown Road, [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), where it stayed for 73 years before leaving in 1995 to Red Hall in Leeds, a [Grade II listed](/wiki/Grade_II_listed "Grade II listed") brick building dating from 1642. The RFL left Red Hall and Leeds in May 2021 with the building becoming a private hospital.[[37]](#cite_note-Britlist-39)[[38]](#cite_note-RedHall-40)

In 2015, some departments including Super League, moved to offices at Quay West in Trafford Wharf, [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester").[[39]](#cite_note-41)
